name: Bragi
description: Bragi is the Norse god of poetry, music, and eloquence. He is often depicted as an old man with a long, flowing beard, symbolizing wisdom and experience. As the skald of Valhalla, Bragi welcomes fallen warriors with songs and recounts tales of heroism. His role is not merely that of an entertainer, but a preserver of history and cultural memory. Bragi's wife is Idunn, the keeper of the apples of immortality, linking him to themes of eternal youth and the power of artistic expression to transcend mortality. While not a major player in the grand narratives of Ragnarok or the struggles between gods and giants, Bragi's importance lies in his representation of intellectual and artistic pursuits, essential components of Norse society and worldview. His presence underscores the value placed on skillful oration, poetic composition, and the ability to articulate complex ideas effectively, making him a vital figure in the cultural landscape of Norse mythology.
subtitle: God of Poetry

keyMyths.title: Bragi's Arrival in Valhalla
keyMyths.description: When worthy warriors fall in battle and are brought to Valhalla, Bragi is often the first to greet them. He does so not with weapons or shows of strength, but with carefully crafted poems and songs that celebrate their valor and accomplishments. This initial welcome establishes the warrior's place within the halls of the slain and reinforces the importance of their deeds for posterity. Bragi's role highlights the Norse emphasis on remembrance and the power of art to immortalize heroes.

keyMyths.title: Bragi and Loki's Flyting
keyMyths.description: In Lokasenna, Loki engages in a flyting, a verbal contest of insults, with various gods and goddesses. When Loki turns his vitriol towards Bragi, he accuses him of cowardice and lack of skill in battle, suggesting that Bragi is only good at sitting on a bench and reciting poems. Bragi attempts to de-escalate the situation, but Loki persists, revealing the tensions and rivalries that sometimes existed even amongst the gods of Asgard. This episode showcases Bragi's role as a symbol of peace and diplomacy.

keyMyths.title: Bragi and Idunn
keyMyths.description: Bragi is married to Idunn, the goddess who guards the apples of immortality. These apples are crucial for maintaining the youth and vitality of the gods. The connection between Bragi and Idunn underscores the relationship between art and eternal life. Bragi's role as a poet grants him a form of immortality through his works, while Idunn's apples grant physical immortality. Together, they represent the preservation of knowledge, beauty, and vitality.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A 13th-century Icelandic prose work that recounts Norse mythology, including details about Bragi's role as the god of poetry.
sources.text: A 13th-century Icelandic prose work that recounts Norse mythology, including details about Bragi's role as the god of poetry.

sources.author: John Lindow
